content.text: Synchronous code runs one task at a time — and for many programs, that is a surprisingly expensive constraint
When a Python program reads a file, queries a database, or fetches data from an API, it spends most of its time

. The CPU sits idle while the operating system waits for a response from disk hardware, a network socket, or an external service. Traditional, synchronous Python simply blocks during that wait — no other work gets done until the response arrives. This is the core inefficiency that asynchronous programming is designed to eliminate.
Asynchronous programming is a concurrency model where a single thread can
a task that is waiting for something external and
to another task that is ready to make progress — all without creating new threads or processes.
In Python, this model is built into the standard library through the
module, introduced in Python 3.4 and significantly matured by Python 3.7+. The programming style uses two keywords —
— to mark functions that can be paused and resumed cooperatively.

refers to tasks whose speed is limited by input/output operations rather than by CPU computation. Reading a file, making an HTTP request, querying a database, or receiving a message from a queue are all I/O-bound. In contrast, computing the digits of pi or training a neural network is
— it is limited by processor speed, not waiting time.
Async is specifically designed to solve the I/O-bound problem.
To understand why async matters, it helps to see what synchronous code does with I/O time.
Consider fetching 100 URLs one after another:

import requests

urls = [f"https://example.com/api/{i}" for i in range(100)]

for url in urls:
    response = requests.get(url)  # blocks here, maybe 200ms per request
    print(response.status_code)
If each request takes 200 milliseconds, the total runtime is roughly

. During each of those 200ms windows, the CPU is not computing anything — it is simply waiting. Multiply this pattern across a web server handling thousands of simultaneous clients, and the inefficiency becomes severe.

Async programming reclaims that idle waiting time. Instead of blocking, the program registers that it is waiting, sets a task aside, and immediately picks up another task. When the original response arrives, the task is resumed exactly where it left off.
The most intuitive analogy is a

A synchronous waiter takes one table's order, walks to the kitchen, stands there waiting for the food to be prepared, carries it back, then goes to the next table. One table at a time, most of the time spent waiting.

An asynchronous waiter takes one table's order, submits it to the kitchen, then immediately goes to the next table while the kitchen works. When the food is ready, a signal comes back and the waiter picks it up. The waiter is always doing useful work; the kitchen's preparation time is handled in parallel even though there is only one waiter.
the waiter is the CPU thread, the kitchen is the external I/O system, and the tables are concurrent tasks
. Async does not make the kitchen faster — it keeps the waiter from standing around idle.
Python's async model is built on an
. This is a scheduler that runs inside a single OS thread and manages a collection of coroutines.
. They are not regular functions — when called, they return a coroutine object rather than executing immediately. They only execute when handed to the event loop.
, it tells the event loop: "I am waiting for this result; you can run something else in the meantime."
Here is the lifecycle in plain terms:
The event loop maintains a queue of runnable tasks.
It picks a task and runs it until the task hits an
The task is suspended and its state (local variables, position in code) is saved.
The event loop picks another runnable task and runs it.
When the I/O the first task was waiting for completes, the OS notifies the event loop.
The first task is put back in the runnable queue and eventually resumes from exactly where it paused.
points rather than being forcibly interrupted by the OS (which is what threads do).
Architecture: The Event Loop and Coroutine Lifecycle
only one coroutine runs at any given instant

. There is no true parallelism here. The performance gain comes from the fact that I/O wait time — which could be 90%+ of total time — is now shared productively across many concurrent tasks instead of being wasted.
I/O-Bound vs CPU-Bound: A Clear Distinction
This distinction is fundamental to understanding when async helps and when it does not.
HTTP requests, DB queries, file reads
Image processing, ML training, sorting large datasets
Partially (GIL limits Python threads for CPU work)
never fires in a meaningful way — the coroutine just runs straight through without pausing. Async adds overhead without benefit. For CPU-bound work, Python's

A single async Python process can manage tens of thousands of concurrent connections. Threads, by contrast, consume several megabytes of stack memory each — managing 10,000 threads is not practical. Coroutines are lightweight Python objects with minimal overhead.
Simpler mental model for network code.

Once the async/await syntax is learned, code reads top-to-bottom like synchronous code, even though it is concurrent. Callback-based async patterns (common in older JavaScript or Python's Twisted library) force the developer to fragment logic across many small functions.
No race conditions from shared memory
(in most cases). Because only one coroutine runs at a time, and switches only happen at
points, it is generally safe to read and modify shared state without locks. This removes an entire category of bugs that plague multi-threaded programs.
Scales with I/O, not thread count.
The number of concurrent operations is limited by OS file descriptors and network connections, not by thread counts or memory. This maps well to web servers, API clients, and data pipelines.
Only one CPU core is used.
The event loop runs on a single thread. A CPU-intensive task will block the entire event loop, freezing all other coroutines. Mixing CPU-heavy work into an async program requires careful use of
to offload work to a thread pool or process pool.

propagate upward through the call stack. If a low-level function is async, every caller must also be async. This can require refactoring large codebases and makes it awkward to mix synchronous and asynchronous code.
Standard synchronous libraries block the event loop. Using
inside an async function blocks every other coroutine. Async I/O requires async-compatible libraries:
. Not every library has an async version.
Stack traces in async code can be harder to read because the call stack is managed by the event loop scheduler rather than the OS. Tools like
and Python 3.11+'s improved task introspection help, but async bugs — particularly around cancellation and exception handling — require more care.
Async is the right choice when:
The program makes many network requests concurrently (web scraping, API clients, microservice communication).
Building a web server or API that must handle many simultaneous clients (FastAPI, aiohttp, Sanic are all async-first).
Reading or writing many files concurrently (log processors, batch file converters).
Connecting to databases with high query volumes where connection latency dominates.
Building a WebSocket server or chat system where many connections are open simultaneously.
Async is the wrong choice when:
The codebase is simple and sequential with little concurrency needed (synchronous code is easier to understand and debug).
The libraries required have no async versions and wrapping them in executors would add more complexity than benefit.
The program runs a single task from start to finish with no concurrent I/O.
This is the simplest possible demonstration of what async buys over synchronous code.
Synchronous version — tasks block each other:

import time

def fetch(name, delay):
    print(f"Starting {name}")
    time.sleep(delay)  # simulates I/O wait
    print(f"Done {name}")

start = time.time()
fetch("A", 2)
fetch("B", 2)
fetch("C", 2)
print(f"Total: {time.time() - start:.1f}s")
# Output: Total: 6.0s

import asyncio
import time

async def fetch(name, delay):
    print(f"Starting {name}")
    await asyncio.sleep(delay)  # yields control during the wait
    print(f"Done {name}")

async def main():
    start = time.time()
    await asyncio.gather(
        fetch("A", 2),
        fetch("B", 2),
        fetch("C", 2),
    )
    print(f"Total: {time.time() - start:.1f}s")

asyncio.run(main())
# Output: Total: 2.0s
— all three start, all three wait simultaneously, and all three finish together. Total elapsed time is the length of the longest single wait, not the sum of all waits.
is the key function here: it schedules multiple coroutines to run concurrently and waits for all of them to finish.
blocks the entire event loop — it does not yield.
is the async-safe equivalent that properly suspends the coroutine.
A realistic async HTTP client using

import asyncio
import aiohttp
import time

async def fetch_url(session, url):
    async with session.get(url) as response:
        data = await response.json()
        return data

async def main():
    urls = [f"https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/{i}" for i in range(1, 101)]

    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [fetch_url(session, url) for url in urls]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

    print(f"Fetched {len(results)} posts")

start = time.time()
asyncio.run(main())
print(f"Elapsed: {time.time() - start:.2f}s")
In production, this pattern fetches all 100 URLs concurrently. A synchronous version using

in a loop would take roughly 20–30 seconds for the same 100 requests at typical API latency. The async version completes in 1–3 seconds — not because the network is faster, but because all requests are in-flight simultaneously.
A production version would add error handling, rate limiting (to avoid overwhelming the target server), timeout settings, and retry logic — but the core concurrency pattern is exactly as shown above.
Using blocking calls inside async functions.
, or any synchronous I/O inside an
function blocks the entire event loop. Every other coroutine freezes until the blocking call returns. Always use async-compatible equivalents:
does not execute it — it creates a coroutine object and does nothing else. Python 3.x will usually emit a
, but this is easy to miss and leads to silently skipped logic.

# Wrong — this does nothing
result = fetch_data()

# Correct
result = await fetch_data()
Running the event loop manually when
is the correct, clean entry point. It creates a new event loop, runs the coroutine, and cleanly shuts down.
Assuming async means parallel CPU execution.
Async achieves concurrency, not parallelism. Two coroutines do not run simultaneously on two CPU cores. If code performs heavy computation between
points, it will starve other coroutines.
By default, if one task in
raises an exception, the exception propagates but
to collect both results and exceptions, then inspect them individually.
It handles event loop creation and cleanup correctly, including running finalizers and closing the loop on exit.
where many independent tasks must complete before proceeding. Use
when tasks must be started and potentially cancelled or awaited independently.
Set timeouts on every I/O operation.
External services can hang indefinitely. Wrap awaitable calls with
Offload CPU-bound work to an executor.
If a computation must happen inside an async context, use
to run it in a thread pool without blocking the event loop.

import asyncio

def compute_heavy(n):
    return sum(range(n))

async def main():
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    result = await loop.run_in_executor(None, compute_heavy, 10_000_000)
    print(result)
Structure code around async context managers
) for resources like HTTP sessions and database connections. These ensure cleanup happens correctly even when exceptions occur.
A well-written async Python application can handle
tens of thousands of concurrent connections
within a single process. This is comparable to Node.js and Go's goroutines, and far beyond what thread-per-connection models can achieve in Python.
The performance ceiling is set by:
— async does not make the network faster.
— the OS limits how many sockets can be open simultaneously (tunable with
— scheduling thousands of tasks adds some overhead, but it is generally negligible compared to network latency.
— the more computation a coroutine does without yielding, the less responsive the event loop becomes for other tasks.
and regularly benchmark at 20,000–50,000+ requests per second on modest hardware, handling far more than thread-based WSGI servers like Flask with Gunicorn under equivalent concurrency conditions.

Python 3.11 introduced significant performance improvements to the event loop internals, and Python 3.12 further improved task scheduling. Using a recent Python version is worthwhile for async-heavy workloads.

Threads also allow I/O concurrency and work with synchronous libraries without changes. However, threads consume significant memory (each has its own stack), require explicit locking for shared state, and are limited by Python's Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) for CPU work. Async is generally more efficient for large numbers of concurrent I/O tasks, but threads are simpler when dealing with libraries that have no async version.

Processes bypass the GIL and achieve true parallelism, making them the right tool for CPU-bound work. They are heavy — each process has its own memory space and startup cost. They cannot share memory directly. Async does not compete with multiprocessing; they solve different problems.
A higher-level interface over both threads and processes.

is practical for offloading blocking I/O calls without rewriting them as async. It does not provide the lightweight concurrency of coroutines but is simpler to retrofit into existing synchronous codebases.
These libraries monkey-patch the standard library to make synchronous calls behave asynchronously under the hood. They allow using
without changes, which is their main appeal. However, they rely on implicit magic and make debugging harder. Native
is now preferred for new projects because its behavior is explicit and well-integrated with the language.
Async programming is the solution to I/O-bound concurrency: reclaiming CPU time that would otherwise be wasted waiting for networks, disks, and databases.
uses a single-threaded event loop and cooperative coroutines — tasks yield at
points, and the loop runs other tasks in the meantime.
The performance gain is not about making I/O faster; it is about running many I/O operations
so their waiting times overlap instead of stacking.
Async does not help CPU-bound tasks — those require multiprocessing.
Async requires async-compatible libraries throughout the stack; blocking calls inside coroutines freeze the entire event loop.
are the four building blocks needed to write practical async programs.

Modern Python async frameworks (FastAPI, aiohttp) can handle tens of thousands of concurrent requests per second on a single process, making async the foundation of high-performance Python network services.
